coredata.dc:title: Functional composite films incorporating triphala-derived carbon dots for extending chicken preservation

coredata.dc:description: Triphala-based carbon dots (T-CDs) were successfully prepared using a simple one-step hydrothermal method. T-CDs were characterized by absorbance, fluorescence, Fourier-transform infrared, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy. T-CDs showed bright blue fluorescence at 434 nm upon excitation at 360 nm. Functional composite films were prepared using poly(vinyl alcohol) and gelatin mixture by incorporating T-CDs and applied as a packaging film to extend the shelf life of chicken. The antibacterial activity of T-CDs against Listeria monocytogenes and Staphylococcus aureus was evaluated using well diffusion and colony count methods. T-CDs were evenly dispersed throughout the PVA/Gel solution to form a dense and uninterrupted film. They also formed strong bonds with polymer chains, which improved the tensile strength of the film from 32.44 to 42.70 MPa. Furthermore, the presence of T-CDs significantly enhanced the UV-blocking ability of the PVA/Gel films, achieving 99.7 % for UV-B and 97.2 % for UV-A. In addition, the PVA/Gel/T-CDs composite films showed excellent antioxidant, antimicrobial and UV-barrier properties, extending the shelf life of chicken. Therefore, the PVA/Gel/T-CDs composite films showed great potential as an active food packaging material to extend the shelf life and preserve the visual quality of packaged meat.

head.ce:title: Functional composite films incorporating triphala-derived carbon dots for extending chicken preservation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Triphala-based carbon dots (T-CDs) were successfully prepared using a simple one-step hydrothermal method. T-CDs were characterized by absorbance, fluorescence, Fourier-transform infrared, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy. T-CDs showed bright blue fluorescence at 434 nm upon excitation at 360 nm. Functional composite films were prepared using poly(vinyl alcohol) and gelatin mixture by incorporating T-CDs and applied as a packaging film to extend the shelf life of chicken. The antibacterial activity of T-CDs against Listeria monocytogenes and Staphylococcus aureus was evaluated using well diffusion and colony count methods. T-CDs were evenly dispersed throughout the PVA/Gel solution to form a dense and uninterrupted film. They also formed strong bonds with polymer chains, which improved the tensile strength of the film from 32.44 to 42.70 MPa. Furthermore, the presence of T-CDs significantly enhanced the UV-blocking ability of the PVA/Gel films, achieving 99.7 % for UV-B and 97.2 % for UV-A. In addition, the PVA/Gel/T-CDs composite films showed excellent antioxidant, antimicrobial and UV-barrier properties, extending the shelf life of chicken. Therefore, the PVA/Gel/T-CDs composite films showed great potential as an active food packaging material to extend the shelf life and preserve the visual quality of packaged meat.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, petroleum-based synthetic plastic materials have been widely used due to their low cost, lightweight nature, ease of use, resistance to chemical aging and strong mechanical properties [1,2]. The raw materials of plastic are derived from petrochemical monomers, are not biodegradable and are harmful to the environment [3]. The use of polymer-based plastics as food packaging materials is increasing every year and this increase is harming our environment, which is a major cause of environmental pollution, including declining soil health, changing climate systems and degradation of water quality. This affects the well-being of living organisms [1,2,4]. Extensive research has been conducted to develop environmental friendly biopolymer-based plastics as an alternative to synthetic polymers [5–7]. Biopolymers derived from biological sources have several advantages over petroleum-based polymers, including biodegradability, renewability, biocompatibility, cost-effectiveness, and non-toxicity. These properties of biopolymers make them highly sustainable and environmentally friendly [4]. Additionally, functional additives, antioxidants, and antimicrobials can be incorporated into biopolymer-based films to extend their shelf life and enhance food preservation. This ability to enhance food preservation makes biopolymers-based packaging suitable for a wider range of applications [8].

Food waste is a major concern for the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO). This is not only an ethical and economic problem but also affects the environment by consuming limited natural resources such as water, energy chemicals and materials [9]. Billions of tons of food have been wasted in recent years. Half of this waste is mainly generated by households in developed countries and the other half is generated due to expiration dates. Each participant in the food supply chain such as producers, processors, retailers and consumers, plays an important role in reducing food waste, and the significant amount of waste generated by households indicates that innovative packaging solutions can be key to reducing waste. Packaging is now an essential technological development that plays a vital role in maintaining food safety, preventing undesirable reactions, meeting consumer demands, and extending the shelf life of food. Despite significant advances in packaging methods and technology, the fundamental goals of packaging have not changed. However, emerging trends such as active and intelligent packaging systems offer real opportunities to reduce food waste. Therefore, the food sector is looking for biocompatible and antibacterial materials to extend food shelf life [9]. Increasing demands for food safety and security require the use of functionally active ingredients in active food packaging applications [10]. To solve the food crisis that is deepening due to rapid population growth and resource depletion, strategies are needed to prevent food spoilage or deterioration and extend the food shelf life [10]. Active food packaging containing active antibacterial and antioxidant agents is essential to protect foods from oxidative damage and microbial contamination [11].

Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) is a hydrophilic synthetic biodegradable polymer produced by first polymerizing vinyl acetate monomer into polyvinyl acetate and then converting it to PVA through a controlled saponification process. This process partially replaces the ester groups of vinyl acetate with hydroxyl groups in a sodium hydroxide medium, thus the degree of hydrolysis of PVA is determined by the saponification reaction conditions. PVA has received considerable attention due to its excellent mechanical strength, high transparency, chemical stability, biocompatibility, extensive hydrophilicity, non-toxicity, superior film-forming ability, and complete biodegradability [12]. Gelatin (Gel), a biopolymer derived from animal protein, is mainly produced through denaturation and hydrolysis of collagen [13]. Due to its excellent gelling and thickening abilities, gelatin has wide applications in the food industry. Additionally, films based on gelatin are often used in food packaging due to their superior gas barrier properties and mechanical strength. A common strategy to improve their properties involves modification with two biopolymers [14]. The blending of PVA and Gel has improved tensile strength, biocompatibility, optical transparency, biodegradability, thermal stability, and bioactivity. Additionally, blending Gel and PVA is particularly effective in making binary blend films applicable to food packaging [15].

The demand for sustainable and eco-friendly packaging has prompted extensive research into alternative material technologies. Highly efficient single, composite, or hybrid materials are reinforced with select polymers to obtain desirable packaging materials for extending the shelf life of food [16–18]. Carbon dots (CDs), a novel type of carbon-based nanomaterial, can be used as an additive in various polymers to obtain wholesome materials with enhanced functionality. The nanostructure, surface charges, biocompatibility, and excellent biological properties of CDs that are obtained through green synthesis provide an inexpensive and non-toxic material to increase food shelf life [19].

Triphala is a traditional herbal formulation derived from three medicinal plants Emblica officinalis (amla), Terminalia bellirica (bibhitaki), and Terminalia chebula (haritaki) and it has many bioactive compounds and diverse pharmacological properties [20]. Utilizing Triphala as a precursor for the green synthesis of CDs presents a novel approach consistent with the principles of sustainability and green chemistry. The scalable synthesis of CDs from Triphala herbal mixture (T-CDs) as a source involves carbonization and subsequent functionalization of the organic groups present in the T-CDs. The hydrothermal method offers several distinct advantages for T-CDs production, particularly due to its simple and green synthesis. This thermally mediated approach typically uses a pressurized autoclave, with reaction tempreatures ranging from 120 to 240 °C and reaction times of 3 to 12 h. The method facilitates a one-step synthetic process, controlled size and morphology of the T-CDs, high yield and purity, and efficient incorporation of functional groups [20]. Therefore, T-CDs have antibacterial, antioxidant and UV barrier properties and are used as sensors for the authentication and traceability of food [21]. One promising way to utilize green-synthetic T-CDs is through their application in packaging materials. The packaging industry is undergoing a major shift towards developing sustainable and environmentally friendly materials to reduce the environmental impact of packaging waste. T-CDs offer an attractive solution due to their biocompatibility, low toxicity, and potential antimicrobial activities, which can extend the shelf life of packaged products and mitigate the risk of foodborne illness.

Based on our knowledge, there is no research on T-CDs and their application to extend the shelf life of food as an active packaging material. In this present study, T-CDs emitting blue fluorescence were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. The resultant T-CDs exhibited fluorescence properties dependent on the excitation wavelength, with emission observed at 434 nm when excited at 360 nm. The structure and morphology of the T-CDs were characterized through analysis including FT-IR, XPS, and HR-TEM. Further investigations were carried out to explore the possible mechanisms that explain T-CDs antibacterial activity and their effectiveness in preserving the safety and quality of food packaging applications. The successful incorporation of naturally synthesized T-CDs from Triphala packaging materials via the proposed method represents a sustainable and eco-friendly application in food packaging.

Triphala herb mix powder used as raw material for T-CDs synthesis was purchased from an online market. The tender chicken meat for packaging application was purchased from a local supermarket (Seoul, Korea). Food-grade gelatin (200 Bloom, Type A) was obtained from Gel-Tec Co. Ltd. (Seoul, Korea). Poly(vinyl alcohol) (∼99 % hydrolyzed and MW of 146,000–186,000 kDa), 2,2′-azinobis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Glycerol, ascorbic acid, and other reagents were purchased from Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co., Ltd. (Siheung, Korea). Foodborne pathogens such as L. monocytogenes ATCC 15313 and S. aureus ATCC 6538 were provided by the Korean Collection for Type Culture (KCTC, Seoul, Korea).

Triphala-derived CDs (T-CDs) were prepared using one-step hydrothermal method with slight modification to a previously reported procedure (Fig. 1 ) [20]. Triphala powder (1 g) was dispersed in 30 mL distilled water, transferred to a 100 mL Teflon cylinder and heated at 170 °C for 7 h. After the reaction was completed, the resulting solution was cooled and centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 25 min to remove unreacted substances. The supernatant was filtered through a membrane filter (diameter of 25 mm and a pore size of 0.22 μm) to remove impurities. The final solution was stored at 8 °C for further use.

Both antioxidant and antibacterial activities of T-CDs were determined in a dose-dependent pattern. DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging assays were adopted to evaluate antioxidant activity [22]. The change in absorbance when 10 mL DPPH and ABTS solutions were added to the test samples was determined after incubation at room temperature for 30 min. Ascorbic acid (100 μg/mL) was used as a standard. A reaction solution without the sample is used as the control for the experiment. Subsequently, the absorbance values were recorded using a UV–vis absorbance at 517 and 734 nm, respectively. Antioxidant activity was calculated using the Eq. (1) as follows:
(1)

Initially, antibacterial tests were performed by Agar well diffusion against six bacterial pathogens. To validate the efficiency of T-CDs, two samples of bacteria from both Gram-positive and negative were selected, and the dose-dependent antibacterial activity of T-CDs against these pathogens was investigated. For this study, approximately 25 mL of different concentrations of

T-CDs (0.10, 0.25, 0.50, and 1 mg/mL) were prepared in nutrient broth. To this working solution, 500 μL of bacteria was inoculated from the stocks. The broth was incubated at 30 °C for 24 h under shaking conditions (250 rpm/min). After an incubation period, samples were diluted and bacterial populations were determined by the spread plate technique. Antimicrobial rates were determined based on the colony count using the following Eq. (2):
(2)

%

where B

t are the bacterial population in control and in the treatment with T-CDs.

PVA/Gel-based composite films were prepared via the solution casting method [23]. The two polymers were mixed 1:1 with mechanical stirring. T-CDs were incorporated into the polymer blend at three different concentrations of 1 %, 3 % and 5 % (based on polymer) to increase the functional properties. After thorough mixing to ensure homogeneity, the film solution was immediately poured onto a glass plate covered with Teflon (24 × 30 cm) and left to cure at 25 °C for 48 h. Afterward, the dried films were peeled off the plate and conditioned at 25 °C and 50 % relative humidity for 48 h before further analysis.

The surface and cross-sectional morphology of the film samples were analyzed using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, SU8010, Hitachi Co., Ltd., S4 Matsuda, Japan) operating at an accelerating voltage of 8 kV. Characterization of the functional groups was performed by acquiring Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra within the spectral range of 4000 to 500 cm−1 (ATR PRO 4X FTIR spectrophotometer, Jasco, Tokyo, Japan).

The surface color of the films was evaluated using a Chromo meter (CR-400 Chromameter, Konica Minolta, Tokyo, Japan) [24]. The UV barrier and transparency of the films were evaluated by measuring the light transmittance at 280 an d 660 nm wavelengths using a spectrophotometer (POP Series UV–vis, Mecasys Optizen, Seoul, Korea). The mechanical properties of the films were evaluated using a Universal Testing Machine according to ASTM D 882-88 standard. The initial grip separation was set to 50 mm and the crosshead speed was maintained at 5 mm/min. At least 10 specimens were successfully tested and the average values were reported.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA)
ce:section.ce:para: Water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) of the film was evaluated using the gravimetric cup method according to the ASTM E96-95 standard procedure. Additionally, the surface wettability of the films was analyzed using a contact angle analyzer (Phoneix 150, Surface Electro-Optics Co., Ltd., Kunpo, Korea) [24].

Real-time quality monitoring of chicken tender samples during storage was evaluated by inoclulating frish chicken. The inoculated samples were dried under sterile conditions and then warpped in neat PVA/Gel film and PVA/Gel/T-CDs 5 % film. The packaged samples were sealed in polyethyene bages and stored at 4 °C for 15 days and quality changes were assessed every three days. The bacterial efficacy of the films was evaluated by determining bacterial populations according to the reported method [11]. The packaging tests were performed in triplicate and the average values were reported. Thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) were measured using the method [25]. Chicken samples were homogenized in 7.5 % trichloriactic acid and extracted by continuous shaking at 50 °C for 0.5 h. The homogenate was filtered and 5 mL of the filtrate was mixed with 0.02 M 2-thiobarbituric acid in equal ratio, incubated at 50 °C for 0.5 h and then cooled to sufficient temperature. The TBARS value was calculated using a standard method. In addition, the change in surface color of chicken samples wrapped with films was visually observed. Bacterial growth and quality parameters such as pH, water activity, and TBARS were also determined [25].

All experiments were performed in triplicate and results were presented as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical analysis was performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) to assess significant differences between film properties (p < 0.05). Statistical analysis was conducted using the SPSS statistical analysis program (SPSS 21, SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

T-CDs exhibits a distinct absorption band at ∼360 nm (Fig. 2(a)). It is formed due to the electron transition from the non-bonding electron pair to an anti-bonding π-π* orbital, which causes the n-π* transition of the CO bonds [20,26]. T-CDs showed a distinct emission peak at 434 nm upon excitation at 360 nm, indicating the formation of T-CDs with blue fluorescence. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 2(b) T-CDs showed an excitation band at 360 nm when the emission was set at 434 nm. The aqueous dispersion of T-CDs appeared colorless in visible light, but upon irradiation with UV light, it produced blue color fluorescence (shown in the inset Fig. 2(b(i&ii)). Excitation dependent fluorescence emission was studied by varying the excitation wavelengths between 300 and 450 nm. The emission intensity initially increased at 300 nm, peaked at a maximum intensity at 360 nm, and then gradually decreased with further increase in wavelength (Fig. S1).

The surface functional groups and chemical composition of green synthetic T-CDs were characterized by FT-IR and XPS spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectra of Triphala extract and T-CDs are shown in Fig. S2. Comparative analysis of the FT-IR peaks between Triphala extract and T-CDs revealed several distinct peaks due to the transformation and rearrangements of chemical groups and chemical bonds. Triphala extract contains -OH, CC, and NH groups. On the other hand, an absorption peak was observed at 3325 cm−1 in T-CDs, which corresponds to the stretching vibration of OH, indicating the presence of surface hydrophilic -OH [27]. The band observed at 2918–2851 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibration of -CH group. The stretching vibration peak observed at 1617 cm−1 is attributed to the CC bonds, which represents aryl and carboxyl groups, respectively [28]. The peak at 1514 cm−1 represents -NH group, suggesting the presence of amino group [29].

The XPS spectra of T-CDs are shown in Fig. 3 . The survey spectrum of T-CDs shows three peaks at 283.66, 398.74, and 531.24 eV, which correspond to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively (Fig. 3(a)). According to the XPS analysis, the chemical composition of T-CDs was 67.05 % carbon, 1.23 % nitrogen, and 31.72 % oxygen. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s shows binding energies of 283.66, 284.70, and 287.48 eV, which correspond to CC/CC, CO/CN, and HOCO bonds, respectively (Fig. 3(b)) [30]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3(c)) shows two peaks at 398.74 and 400.28 due to the nitrogen in the form of pyridinic nitrogen CNC and pyrrolic nitrogen CNH, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3(d), the O 1s spectrum shows two typical peaks at approximately 531.24 and 530.40 eV. These peaks correspond to the hydroxyl (CHO) and adsorbed oxygen (COC) bonds, respectively [21,31]. The results of FT-IR and XPS suggest that T-CDs are composed of functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups. The presence of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of T-CDs shows excellent water solubility and enhanced fluorescence properties. The size and morphology of T-CDs were investigated using HR-TEM measurement, and the results are shown in Fig. 3(e). HR-TEM analysis showed that the T-CDs were nearly spherical and well-dispersed. Size distribution analysis showed that most of the T-CDs were in the range of 3 to 7 nm, with an average size of about 5.5 nm (Fig. 3(f)). The HR-TEM image clearly showed lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.20 nm. This result shows that T-CDs are small, homogeneous, and dispersed in water. In addition, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns showed a broad diffraction peak corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite centered at 2θ = 23.21° (Fig. S3), indicating the amorphous structure of the T-CDs [32,33].

Fluorescence spectroscopy was used to investigate the influence of different physiological conditions on T-CDs. Specifically, the effects of different pH, UV light irradiation and different concentrations of salts were investigated and the results are showed in Figs. S4–6. According to Fig. S4, T-CDs showed higher fluorescence intensity under acidic conditions (pH 2–6) and decreased fluorescence intensity in basic conditions (pH 7–13). The differential behavior of T-CDs may be due to surface charge changes associated with protonation under acidic conditions and deprotonation under basic conditions. Additionally, the photostability of T-CDs was tested through continuous UV irradiation for 60 min and the fluorescence intensity was more stable as shown in Fig. S5. Fluorescence responses to different NaCl concentrations also showed that changes in salt levels did not significantly affect T-CDs fluorescence, indicating the salt tolerance and overall stability of T-CDs under various ionic conditions (Fig. S6).

The DPPH and ABTS assays were used to determine the antioxidant activity of T-CDs samples. The ability of T-CDs to scavenge and neutralize free radicals was evaluated through colorimetric assessment using ascorbic acid as standard [10]. Concentrations of T-CDs were 1, 3, 5, 7, and 10 μg/mL. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the highest antioxidant activity was observed at 10 μg/mL for both DPPH and ABTS assays. In previously reported studies, CDs showed over 90 % antibacterial activity only at high concentrations above 50 μg/mL [34], but T-CDs showed excellent antibacterial activity at the concentration of 10 μg/mL. The antioxidant activity of banana peel CDs estimated by ABTS and DPPH methods were 98.3 and 93.04 % at 100 μg/mL [35]. Compared to the previous studies, T-CDs showed >90 % improved antioxidant activity at a concentration of 10 μg/mL. The antioxidant activity can be attributed to surface oxygenated functional groups. Functional transfer of H from the -OH of CDs quenches ABTS and DPPH to ABTS-H and DPPH-H, highlighting the scavenging effect [35]. In previous studies, salt extract with effective antioxidant properties, has been used as an active agent to extend the shelf life of foods. Extract of the plant Echinophora cinerea, a perennial plant with antioxidant and antibacterial activities, have been used for the preservation of Aristichthys nobilis (Bighead carp) fillets [36]. However, in recent years, direct utilization of the unique benefits of plant extracts has been replaced by CDs prepared from the same plant extracts. These CDs with fluorescent, optical, antioxidant, and antibacterial properties act as suitable active agents in food packaging. The antioxidant activity of CDs obtained from plant sources has been reported previously. Fluorescent CDs derived from papaya plant leaves showed the highest antioxidant activity of 27.6 μg/mL at a half maximal effective concentration (EC50) as assayed by the DPPH method [37]. CDs synthesized from the plant Salvia miltiorrhiza (Red sage) by hydrothermal method possessed excellent antioxidant properties. The antioxidant activities were 71.4, 88.9 and 95.6 % determined by OH, DPPH, and O2 − scavenging tests, respectively [38].

The bactericidal activity of T-CDs was tested against foodborne pathogens. The results of the primary agar well diffusion assay (Fig. 4(b(i-vi))) showed that T-CDs have inhibitory activity against three Gram-positive bacteria (L. monocytogenes, S. aureus and B. cereus) and three Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli, S. enterica and P. aeruginosa). As T-CDs tested have the potential to control for pathogens, each of the Gram-positive and Gram-negative pathogens was selected for determination of antibacterial rates by the spread plate technique. Antibacterial efficacy was evaluated by assessing colony formation in culture across different concentrations of selected T-CDs (0.1, 0.25, 0.5 and 1 mg/mL). These types of results were utilized to determine the effective measurements needed for bacterial activity as shown in Fig. 4(c). There was no antibacterial rate in the control group without T-CDs. It is clear from Fig. 4(c) that the antibacterial rate of T-CDs increased with increasing dosage. The tested pathogens, E. coli and S. aureus exhibited antibacterial rates of 99 % at the highest concentration of 1 mg/mL. At 0.5 mg/mL, the antibacterial activity against test bacteria was over 90 % (93 % for E. coli and 91 % for S. aureus) (Fig. S7).

T-CDs exhibited efficient antibacterial activity against both bacteria, demonstrating that T-CDs are not cell wall specific. Positively charged T-CDs showed antibacterial activity against mixed pathogenic infections caused by E. coli and S. aureus [39]. Although not tested with mixed pathogens, T-CDs can control both Gram-positive and Gram-negative pathogens. The presence of -NH (amino group) deduced from FTIR and XPS can also be considered responsible for the antibacterial activity. Because of the small size of CDs, it is easier for them to penetrate bacterial cells and damage the major intracellular components such as nucleic acids and proteins. They can also inactivate bacterial cells by interfering with their metabolic functions. The ROS generated from CDs can potentially damage cell structure and function [40,41]. CDs synthesized from the base material Curcuma longa (turmeric) powder extract exhibited a broad spectrum of antibacterial activity. When blue light was irradiated on CDs, vegetative cells of E. coli and S. aureus were inactivated. Under dark conditions, 5 μg/mL of turmeric CDs inactivated 51 % and 55 % of S. aureus, respectively, and the inactivation rates against S. aureus increased to 99.9 % under light conditions. Exposure to blue light generates ROS molecules that damage cell membranes and cause cellular component leakage [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structural, surface color, optical and mechanical properties and characterization of PVA/Gel/T-CDs films
ce:section.ce:para: The addition of any additives to biopolymer films changes the appearance, physical, mechanical and functional properties of the films. The packaging films were prepared using equal ratio of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and gelatin (Gel). These biopolymer blend films were functionalized by adding 1 %, 3 % and 5 % T-CDs. The prepared neat PVA/Gel film remained colorless and transparent. Addition of T-CDs to the PVA/Gel film results in a yellow tint due to the canary yellow color of the synthesized T-CDs as shown in Fig. 5(a). A significant decrease in L-value corresponding to brightness from 0 to 100 can be observed. As the concentration of T-CDs was added, the yellowness (b value) and redness (a value) of the film increased. The film containing 5 % T-CDs had the highest b and a values. The yellowness (b value) of the control was 1.79 ± 0.09 whereas the film containing 5 % T-CDs showed 29.42 ± 0.25 of b value. As the a and b values increase, the total color difference (ΔE) of the film increases significantly.

The morphology of the PVA/Gel-based functional films was investigated by FE-SEM (Fig. 5(b&c)). The surface of the PVA/Gel-based films was uniform and crack-free even after incorporation of T-CDs. The two blended polymers had excellent compatibility and formed a dense, stable and strong matrix. Because the T-CDs were dispersed in the biopolymer blend to create a homogenous mixture, the resulting films have no aggregations, gap, or cracks. Despite the uniform surface, the addition of T-CDs provided the film a slightly rougher appearance. Cross-sectional FE-SEM images do not reveal significant structural differences with any cracks, pits, or voids. All these observations suggest that there is a strong interaction of T-CDs along the hydrophilic structure of PVA/Gel, which improves the properties of the composite films.

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to analyze the surface functional groups of PVA/Gel and PVA/Gel/T-CDs films. The FT-IR spectra of PVA/Gel and PVA/Gel/T-CDs composite films are shown in Fig. 6(a). The OH and CH stretching vibrations of PVA/Gel chains were responsible for the transmittance peaks at 3280 and 2935 cm−1 [13]. Additionally, the characteristic peaks around 1446 and 1236 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibrations of CH and NH of the gelatin [13]. For PVA film, the peak at around 1740 cm−1 was assigned to CO stretching, while the peak at around 1446 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of -CH2, which represents the C-O-C stretching of PVA [13,43]. The T-CDs are incorporated into PVA/Gel films, the characteristic peaks overlapped with the corresponding -OH, CH, CO and NH stretching vibrations of PVA/Gel polymer blend within the same spectral region. FT-IR spectra showed reduced peak intensity without changing the frequency region of the film spectrum containing T-CDs. This indicates that blending of T-CDs with PVA/Gel polymer matrix results in compatible films. The crystallinity of the films was measured using X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, and the corresponding XRD spectra are shown in Fig. 6(b). The prominent peaks (2θ = 19.78°, 19.83°, 19.95° and 20.05°) observed in all XRD patterns indicate the presence of a non-typical crystalline structure in the films. Moreover, semi-crystalline PVA films typically exhibit a single broad peak at 2θ = 19.72°, corresponding to the (101) crystal plane [44]. Therefore, the single peak observed in this study may be due to the interaction between PVA and Gel molecules, which may affect the crystalline structure of the blend film. Additionally, both PVA and Gel are semi-crystalline materials containing crystalline and amorphous regions. The XRD pattern of the blend films showed a noticeable peak at 2θ = 19.78°, which was mainly attributable to the presence of PVA rather than Gel. When PVA and Gel were mixed, the crystalline structure of the film showed an increase in brightness and a shift in the boundary compared to the individual components. The incorporation of T-CDs facilitated chain formation between PVA and Gel, resulting in structural rearrangement within the film. In particular, increasing the T-CDs concentration corresponding to silty increases crystallinity and makes the interaction between PVA/Gel and T-CDs more effective within the film matrix.

The optical properties, transparency and UV-blocking properties of the films can reveal their functionality is shown in Fig. 6(c&d). Light transmittance through the PVA/Gel film (control) was high, above 400 nm in visible light. The control film transmitted 88 % light, but as the T-CDs ratio increased, the light transmittance of the film gradually decreased. The transmittances of the films were 87 % for PVA/Gel/T-CDs 1 %, 70 % for PVA/Gel/T-CDs 3 %, and 58 % for PVA/Gel/T-CDs 5 %. As a result, the film with T-CDs remained transparent, but the light transmission ability of the film decreased. Analysis of the UV-blocking properties of the film showed that adding T-CDs to the films significantly increased the UV-blocking properties of the film. When tested at 660 nm, the control film showed a blocking ability of 32.48 %, and as the T-CDs increased, the UV blocking ability of the film also increased. The UV transmittance of the film drastically decreased to 3.27 % when 1 % of T-CDs were added, and to 0.1 % when 3 % and 5 % of T-CDs were added. The light scattering tendency and light absorption ability of T-CDs contribute to the UV barrier properties of the film [45]. UV and light blocking properties of packaging films are considered important because some sensitive ingredients in foods, such as vitamins, undergo decomposition reactions when exposed to light [46]. Adding T-CDs to PVA/Gel film can improve the optical properties of the film and develop a packaging film that prevents food from being oxidized or decomposed by light. Modified films containing chitosan/gelatin combined with banana peel CDs increased the UV-blocking properties of the films [35].

The transparency and optical properties of packaging films play an important role in ensuring visibility and satisfying consumer expectations. Incorporation of active nanofillers into biopolymers-based films is a well-established method for imparting active functional properties [13,47], however, they frequently change the appearance of films. Therefore, it is essential to evaluate the color and optical properties of packaging materials. The color properties and light transmittance of neat PVA/Gel film and PVA/Gel/T-CDs films are shown in Table 1 . The lightness of neat PVA/Gel film decreased depending on the addition of T-CDs while a-value and b-value were found to increase significantly (p < 0.05) with the addition of T-CDs. Therefore, the total color difference (ΔE) increased significantly (p < 0.05) with T-CDs. The yellowness of the films containing T-CDs was due to the color of T-CDs (Fig. 5(a)). As shown in Table 1, the transparency and UV-barrier properties of the films were evaluated by measuring their light transmittance at T660 and T280 nm wavelengths. Neat PVA/Gel film exhibited fascinating UV-blocking properties with a transmittance of 32.48 ± 0.03 % at T280 nm and high transparency with a transmission of 89.84 ± 0.12 % at T660 nm. On the other hand, the addition of 5 % T-CDs showed only 0.10 % transmittance at T280 and a significantly (p < 0.05) decreased to 58.54 ± 0.44 % at T660. This means that the films containing T-CDs completely block UV radiation while slightly sacrificing its transparency. Several factors affect the UV-blocking ability of T-CDs, including the size of the π-π* and n-π* conjugated domain, the nature and composition of surface functional groups and the O/N ratio within the carbon core [48]. Intramolecular proton transport through O-H-N, O-H-O tunnels, and appropriate junction structures provide the UV barrier properties of T-CDs-added films. Transparent films of PVA/Gel/T-CDs with excellent UV blocking can potentially be used in food packaging applications as a packaging material to prevent chemical reactions in food caused by UV light [45].

Table 2 shows the mechanical properties, water vapor permeability, thickness, and water contact angle of the PVA/Gel and PVA/Gel/T-CDs films. The thickness of the control PVA/Gel film was around 62.17 ± 1.56 μm and increased to 69.67 ± 0.23 μm by the addition of T-CDs. The PVA/Gel film was relatively strong and stiff with low flexibility and its tensile strength (TS), elongation at break (EB), and modulus of elasticity (EM) were 32.44 ± 0.89 MPa, 29.79 ± 2.19 %, and 0.74 ± 0.01GPa, respectively. However, adding T-CDs significantly (p < 0.05) decreased TS while its EB and EM were significantly (p < 0.05) increased. Water vapor permeability (WVP) of PVA/Gel-based films was increased significantly (p < 0.05) with the addition of T-CDs. This effect can be attributed to the hydrophilic nature of T-CDs, which increases the number of hydrophilic hydroxyl groups within the filled matrix, thereby increasing interactions with water molecules. Previous studies have reported similar results that adding CDs to cellulose and Gel-based films increased their WVP. The water contact angle (WCA) of the PVA/Gel film was 73.15 ± 0.32, indicating a hydrophobic surface [13]. However, the addition of T-CDs substantially (p < 0.05) reduced the hydrophobicity of the film due to the hydrophilic properties of T-CDs. Therefore, the addition of T-CDs in various biopolymer-based films reduces WCA [13]. The increased solubility percentage of the composite films is likely attributed to the surface hydrophilic nature of the T-CDs. These properties are expected to enhance the release of active components within the film, thereby providing advantageous antimicrobial and antioxidant activities [19,41].

Based on the physical, chemical and functional properties of the composite films, the PVA/Gel/T-CDs-5 % film was selected for the development of food packaging films. A real packaging test was performed using a chicken tender in a lab-scale experiment and the quality changes of the chicken tender were shown in Fig. 7 . For packaging test, soft tender chicken pieces were wrapped with the neat PVA/Gel film and PVA/Gel/T-CDs-5 % composite film. Changes in the appearance and physical quality of the meat were evaluated during 15 days of storage at 4 °C. Chicken samples wrapped with PVA/Gel/T-CDs-5 % film served as better samples after the storage period. However, the redness of the treatment film increased significantly after the third day, when compared to the control. Chicken tenders that had passed their storage period were removed from the packaging films and changes in texture were observed. In the control packaging, a sticky white layer was observed on the surface, indicating spoilage due to bacterial growth (Fig. 7). But chicken tenders wrapped with PVA/Gel/T-CDs-5 % film remained fresh without any symptoms of spoilage. To further confirm these findings, other physiochemical properties were investigated such as pH, water activity, bacterial growth, and lipid peroxidation during the meat storage (Fig. 8 (a-e)). Fresh chicken meat has a low number of microorganisms, but over time the number of pathogenic bacterial cells increased, leading to spoilage. Normal bacterial flora utilizes the nutrients of chicken meat such as protein, lipids, and carbohydrates to grow. In the absence of external control gents, bacteria thrive. When packed with the PVA/Gel/T-CDs-5 % film, the bacterial population increased by one Log CFU/mL after 15 days of storage, whereas on the control film, the population increased by 4.0 Log. Consequently, the other parameters also change drastically between the control and treatment. Fresh chicken meat normally has a pH of 6.2–6.3 [49]. The chicken meat lies in the acidic scale but the pH value above 6.7 is considered as spoiled [50,51]. In packages with control film, the pH significantly increased throughout the storage period and on the 15th day, the pH of the sample reached 7, which indicates spoilage. In treatment films the pH post-storage remained 6.2 which indicates no spoilage and infers the freshness of the chicken. The water activity measures the free water in food and is one of the critical parameters determining bacterial growth [52]. The TBARS assay was performed to determine the degradation products of fat molecules in the chicken. The TBARS value is often correlated with the freshness of the meat [53,54]. Oxidation of the lipids causes rancidity which begins when the TBA value is 0.4–0.6 mg of malonaldehyde per kilogram of sample. In control, the TBA value gradually increased and finally reached almost 1 mg MDA/kg, which clearly distinct rancidity. But in the treatment film with PVA/Gel/T-CDs-5 %, until 6 days remained around 0.2 mg MDA/kg after which there is a slight increase. However, after 15 days of storage, the TBA value remained at 0.6 mg MDA/kg, which is well within the optimum limits. Hence packaging the tender chicken with PVA/Gel/T-CDs-5 % delayed bacterial growth and prevented lipid oxidation preventing spoilage and increasing the shelf life. The proposed PVA/Gel/T-CDs-5 % film exhibit significant antibacterial properties, effectively inhibiting the growth of pathogens on food surfaces and thereby reducing the risk of microbial contamination and foodborne illnesses. These films also regulate moisture within the packaging, generating a less favorable environment for spoilage microorganisms and pathogens. Food's nutritional content is preserved by the active packaging, which reduces exposure to light and oxygen. Additionally, the radical scavenging action of the films prevents oxidation reactions, thereby maintaining the quality and safety of fats and oils. The PVA/Gel/T-CDs-5 % active composite films enhance food safety standards in the industry by controlling the environment surrounding food reducing the dependence on specific preservatives and additives. The addition of CDs to packaging films improves the physical, chemical, and mechanical properties of the packaging without affecting the film and food quality. CDs further improved the quality and freshness of the foods [40]. Carboxymethyl cellulose films primed with plant extract of Anethum graveolens have been used to preserve fried fish fillets [55–57]. The PVA/Gel/T-CDs composite films in this study showed superior preservation performance compared to conventional and advanced packaging materials. Unlike conventional plastic films such as polyethylene (PE) and polyvinyl chloride (PVC), which primarily act as moisture and oxygen barriers [58], these composite films provide active antimicrobial and antioxidant properties, effectively inhibiting the growth of bacteria such as Listeria monocytogenes and Staphylococcus aureus. Compared with modified atmosphere packaging (MAP), which alters the gas composition but lacks direct antimicrobial action [59], the PVA/Gel/T-CDs films provide both a barrier and an active preservative, effectively extending the shelf life of chicken. Additionally, unlike antimicrobial coatings using nanoparticles that raise concerns about toxicity, the naturally derived triphala carbon dots in these films provide a safer and non-toxic. While biodegradable films such as chitosan-based films are eco-friendly and antimicrobial, the PVA/Gel/T-CDs films are superior in terms of photostability and UV protection, making them more versatile for use in light-sensitive foods.

In this study, an active packaging material with antibacterial and antioxidant properties was successfully developed and characterized by incorporating Triphala-derived carbon dots (T-CDs) into poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) and gelatin-based films. The resulting films exhibited strong antioxidant and antibacterial activities, with uniform distribution of T-CDs throughout the polymer blend. The incorporation of 5 % T-CDs significantly enhanced the hydrophilicity, UV-blocking ability, and overall performance of the films. When applied to fresh chicken samples stored at 4 °C for 15 days, the composite films effectively preserved food quality, as evidenced by stable pH levels, reduced thiobarbituric acid reactive substance (TBARS) values, and controlled bacterial growth. These results suggest that PVA/Gel/T-CDs films with 5 % T-CDs offer great potential for active packaging applications, especially in the field of food preservation. In order to apply this research to actual commercialized products, further research is needed on mass production of T-CDs and making them into a film using an extruder.

Thangarasu Sasikumar: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Jeyakumar Saranya Packialakshmi: Validation, Methodology. Su Jung Hong: Validation, Methodology. Seong Yong Ha: Validation, Methodology. Gye Hwa Shin: Writing – review & editing. Jun Tae Kim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
